title: Can Skills Improve Codex’s Data Analysis Capabilities?
gist: A study using the open-source DataCOPE framework shows that automatically extracting reusable 'Skills' from an agent's problem-solving trajectories materially improves Codex GPT-5.5's accuracy on unseen data-analysis tasks, chiefly by making implicit business conventions explicit—but Skills can also entrench wrong interpretations and cannot replace human validation.

themes.theme: The core failure mode Skills target

insights.claim: Codex's data-analysis failures on realistic tasks stem mainly from analytical conventions never stated in the prompt but required by the underlying business logic, so it produces answers that look reasonable and are backed by executable code yet violate the intended rules.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: The bottleneck for agentic analytics is domain-convention encoding, not raw coding ability—favoring tooling that captures implicit rules over bigger base models.
insights.quote: failures often arise from analytical conventions that are not explicitly stated in the prompt but must still be followed to satisfy the underlying business logic.

insights.claim: The central research question is whether turning task rules, field definitions, calculation procedures, and lessons from common failures into reusable Skills can improve Codex's accuracy on similar unseen tasks.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Frames Skills as a transferable-experience layer—an emerging design pattern for agent products beyond prompt engineering.
insights.locator: This leads to the central question

insights.claim: DataCOPE, released as part of the DataMind project, generates Skills from agent trajectories via three components: a Data-Analytic Agent that does the analysis, a Verifier that scores trajectories against reference answers, and a Skill Manager that organizes vetted experience into a SKILL.md file plus optional supporting scripts.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: The verifier-in-the-loop plus skill-manager pattern is a reusable blueprint for self-improving agents that teams can adopt directly from open source.
insights.locator: The framework contains three main components

insights.claim: Skill generation is iterative: in iteration 0 the agent solves training tasks with no Skill and the Skill Manager builds an initial Skill from successes and failures, then from iteration 1 onward the agent reuses and the manager refines that Skill.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Continuous refinement loops are how agent systems accrue durable capability; the marginal value of each iteration is the metric to track.

insights.claim: The experiment used Codex GPT-5.5 as the Data-Analytic Agent and Claude Code Sonnet 4.6 as the Skill Manager, with a supervised reference-answer Verifier, over a training set of 1 DSBio Spatial Neighbor, 8 DABstep Highest Cost, and 6 DABstep Routing tasks.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: A mixed-vendor stack (OpenAI Codex doing analysis, Anthropic Claude managing skills) signals model-agnostic, best-tool-per-role agent architectures becoming normal.
insights.locator: We use Codex GPT-5.5 as the Data-Analytic Agent

insights.claim: On the hidden test set, Skills lifted the scientific DSBio Spatial Neighbor task from 0/4 to 4/4, DABstep Highest Cost from 2/12 to 5/12, and DABstep Routing from 5/12 to 9/12.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Large jumps on transfer tasks (especially 0/4 to 4/4) show experience-extraction can beat prompt tuning, but small denominators and single-run caveat temper enthusiasm.
insights.locator: The test-set results are shown below

insights.claim: All reported numbers come from a single experimental run, and the authors frame their analysis as focused on concrete examples and failure patterns rather than statistical rigor.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Single-run results mean the effect size is directional, not proven—discount claims until multi-seed replication appears.
insights.quote: All numbers come from a single experimental run, and our analysis focuses primarily on concrete examples and failure patterns.
insights.locator: The test-set results are shown below

insights.claim: A key win is making implicit conventions explicit: in DABstep fee rules, aci: [] and merchant_category_code: [] mean the rule applies to all values (not an empty set) and is_credit: null matches both credit and debit, so the Skill stops Codex from guessing these fields.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Encoding schema semantics that data dictionaries omit is where agent reliability is won or lost in enterprise analytics.
insights.quote: aci: [] and merchant_category_code: [] do not represent empty sets in the conventional sense. Instead, they mean that the rule applies to all ACI or MCC values.

insights.claim: Without a Skill, Codex correctly computed spatial cell neighborhoods but repeatedly returned same-type pairs like macrophage_macrophage, missing that the DSBio task only compares pairs of different cell types.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Illustrates that objective-misunderstanding, not computation error, drives many agent failures—arguing for explicit task-definition guardrails.
insights.quote: Without a Skill, however, Codex frequently selects same-type pairs such as macrophage_macrophage.
insights.locator: 2. Clarifying the Actual Objective of the Task

insights.claim: Skills turn complex business analysis into fixed executable procedures—the DABstep Card Scheme Routing solution is codified as nine ordered steps including repricing the same transactions under every candidate scheme rather than only those that originally used a given scheme.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Deterministic step-sequencing is how you stop agents from silently skipping workflow stages—a template for auditable enterprise agents.
insights.locator: 3. Turning Complex Business Workflows into Executable Procedures

insights.claim: Skills convert recurring failures into explicit checklists, e.g. 'always check whether a fee-rule field represents a wildcard before deciding the rule does not apply' and 'only compare pairs of different cell types.'
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Failure-pattern-to-checklist conversion is a cheap, high-leverage reliability technique any agent builder can copy.
insights.locator: 4. Converting Failure Experience into a Checklist

insights.claim: Skills can reinforce incorrect interpretations: after adopting the Skill, Codex became more consistent on MCC fee questions but several distinct test questions converged on the same wrong answer because the Skill had not captured the correct procedure.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Self-improvement loops can amplify systematic error, not just correct behavior—a governance risk for any auto-learning agent deployment.
insights.quote: A Skill may therefore do more than preserve correct behavior: it may also cause the same incorrect behavior to recur more consistently.

insights.claim: The risk of entrenching wrong behavior becomes more pronounced when the training set is too small or covers only a narrow range of cases.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Training-set coverage is the key control variable for safe skill extraction—thin data is actively dangerous, not merely unhelpful.
insights.quote: This risk becomes more pronounced when the training set is too small or covers only a narrow range of cases.

insights.claim: Skills generalize only within similar task distributions, and packing too many substantially different tasks into one Skill introduces conflicting rules that dilute focus—so Skills should be split by task type and the most relevant one selected at execution time.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Argues for a routed library of narrow Skills over one monolithic Skill—a retrieval/selection layer becomes part of the agent stack.
insights.quote: placing too many substantially different tasks into one Skill may introduce multiple unrelated or even conflicting sets of rules.
insights.locator: 2. Skills Apply to Related Tasks, Not Every Task

insights.claim: Skills cannot replace human validation and are unreliable when the question is ambiguous, field definitions are unclear, training coverage is thin, or the test task differs substantially from training tasks.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Human-in-the-loop remains mandatory for high-stakes analytics—caps how far autonomous agents can be trusted in finance and science today.

insights.claim: The authors recommend structuring a data-analysis Skill into three sections—fields and rules, ordered analysis procedure with intermediate checks, and output format (units, decimal precision)—because many Skills fail when these are mixed together and Codex omits steps.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: A concrete authoring template teams can adopt to raise agent reliability without model changes.
insights.locator: 2. Structure the Skill as a Fixed Workflow

insights.claim: Generic reminders like 'check data quality' or 'verify the result carefully' add little value; failure patterns must instead be expressed as explicit executable rules such as 'do not interpret aci: [] as an empty set' or 'do not use yearly statistics when the task requires monthly transaction volume.'
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Specificity beats generic caution in agent instructions—vague guardrails are effectively noise, a lesson for prompt/skill authoring broadly.
insights.quote: generic reminders such as “check data quality” or “verify the result carefully” are usually of limited value.
insights.locator: 3. Convert Common Errors into Concrete Rules

insights.claim: Complex Skills should be paired with supporting scripts: the DABstep Routing Skill's solve_routing.py standardizes date-range parsing, fee-rule matching, settlement-delay matching, and transaction-level fee calculation so Codex need not rewrite the logic each time.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: Offloading deterministic logic to reusable scripts, with the LLM orchestrating, is a reliability pattern that reduces error surface in agent products.
insights.locator: 4. Pair Complex Skills with Supporting Scripts

insights.claim: The authors propose an ideal division of labor: humans define the problem and validate conclusions, Skills preserve domain rules and known failure patterns, and Codex reads data, computes, and organizes the report.
insights.speaker: Ningyu Zhang
insights.so_what: A clear responsibility model for human-agent collaboration that enterprises can use to scope where automation is safe.
